section: Chapter: Weavers, Iron Smelters and Factory Owners

questions.question: Who were weavers?
questions.correct_answer: Weavers were people who made cloth by weaving yarn on looms.
questions.explanation: They produced cotton and silk textiles using traditional methods.

questions.question: Why were Indian textiles famous in the world?
questions.correct_answer: Because of their fine quality, beautiful designs and skilled craftsmanship.
questions.explanation: Indian cotton and silk fabrics were highly demanded in Europe and Asia.

questions.question: What did the East India Company want from Indian weavers?
questions.correct_answer: The Company wanted weavers to produce cloth only for them.
questions.explanation: They forced weavers to sell cloth at low prices.

questions.question: What was a 'dadni' system?
questions.correct_answer: A system where the Company gave advances to weavers to produce cloth.
questions.explanation: In return, weavers had to sell their cloth only to the Company.

questions.question: How did the East India Company control weavers?
questions.correct_answer: By giving advances and forcing them to sell cloth only to the Company.
questions.explanation: This reduced the independence of weavers.

questions.question: Why did many weavers suffer during British rule?
questions.correct_answer: Because they were forced to sell cloth cheaply and faced strict control by the Company.
questions.explanation: They often fell into debt and poverty.

questions.question: What happened to Indian weaving after British machine-made cloth arrived?
questions.correct_answer: Indian handloom weaving declined due to competition from cheap factory cloth.
questions.explanation: British industrial goods flooded Indian markets.

questions.question: What is a loom?
questions.correct_answer: A loom is a machine used for weaving cloth.
questions.explanation: Traditional weavers used handlooms to produce textiles.

questions.question: What role did British factories play in textile production?
questions.correct_answer: Factories produced machine-made cloth in large quantities.
questions.explanation: This reduced the demand for handmade Indian textiles.

questions.question: Why were Indian weavers important in the economy before British rule?
questions.correct_answer: They produced valuable textiles that were exported worldwide.
questions.explanation: Indian textiles were a major part of trade and economy.

notes.title: Indian Weavers
notes.content: Indian weavers were skilled artisans who produced cotton and silk textiles using traditional handlooms.

notes.title: Company Control Over Weavers
notes.content: The East India Company controlled weavers by giving advances and forcing them to sell cloth only to the Company.

notes.title: Dadni System
notes.content: Under the dadni system, weavers received advances but had to sell cloth only to Company agents at low prices.

notes.title: Decline of Handloom Industry
notes.content: The arrival of cheap machine-made cloth from Britain caused the decline of Indian handloom weaving.

notes.title: Industrialization
notes.content: The Industrial Revolution in Britain led to the rise of factories and mass production of textiles.

facts.fact: Indian cotton textiles were highly demanded in Europe before British industrialization.
The East India Company forced many weavers to sell cloth only to Company agents.
Machine-made cloth from Britain became cheaper than handmade Indian textiles.
The decline of Indian weaving was a major economic impact of British colonial rule.
